questions.question: What is the practical difference between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java?
choices: Checked exceptions are caught at runtime; unchecked are caught at compile time.
Checked exceptions must be declared in the method's throws clause and caught or propagated; unchecked exceptions need no declaration.
Unchecked exceptions are subclasses of Error; checked are subclasses of Exception.
There is no functional difference since Java 8.
questions.explanation: Checked exceptions extend Exception (but not RuntimeException) and the compiler enforces a throws clause or catch block. Unchecked exceptions extend RuntimeException (or Error) and propagate freely without declarations - closer to how C# treats all exceptions.

questions.question: You declare three resources in a single try-with-resources statement. The body throws, and resource[1]'s close() also throws. What happens to the close exception?
choices: It replaces the body exception (only the most recent is propagated).
It is attached as a suppressed exception on the body's exception, visible via getSuppressed() and printed in the stack trace.
The JVM logs it to stderr but doesn't include it in the exception object.
questions.explanation: Try-with-resources preserves close exceptions as suppressed exceptions on the original. JVM stack-trace formatting prints them under 'Suppressed: ...' below the main exception. C#'s using historically discarded such exceptions.

questions.question:
Why does this stream pipeline fail to compile, and what's the standard fix?
paths.stream().map(p -> Files.readString(Path.of(p))).toList();
choices: Files.readString isn't a static method; use a Files instance. Fix: instantiate Files.
readString throws checked IOException, but Function (used by map) doesn't declare exceptions. Fix: catch IOException inside the lambda and rethrow as UncheckedIOException.
toList() doesn't exist on Stream. Fix: use collect(Collectors.toList()).
Path.of is not a Path. Fix: use new Path(p).
questions.explanation: The functional interface Function does not allow throwing checked exceptions, so a lambda that throws IOException doesn't satisfy it. The standard fix is to catch IOException inside the lambda and throw a runtime equivalent (UncheckedIOException is built in for exactly this).
